Enter your income to see whether IRMAA applies. Figures use 2026 amounts. The total reflects your Part B premium plus any Part D IRMAA surcharge — your Part D plan's base premium is separate and varies by plan.

Understanding IRMAA

IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) is an extra amount added to your Medicare Part B and Part D premiums if your income is above a certain level. Most people don't pay it — it applies only to higher-income beneficiaries.

Social Security generally determines IRMAA using your tax return from two years earlier. For 2026 premiums, that usually means your 2024 Modified Adjusted Gross Income.

If your income has dropped because of a life-changing event, you may qualify to have your IRMAA reconsidered by filing Form SSA-44 with Social Security.

Life events that may qualify for a reconsideration

Retirement

Work stoppage

Marriage

New filing status

Divorce

Income change

Death of Spouse

Household change

Loss of Pension

Reduced income

Fewer Work Hours

Reduced income

Questions about Medicare premiums?

Free Medicare Review

Frequently Asked Questions

What is IRMAA?
IRMAA stands for Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount — an extra charge added to your Medicare Part B and Part D premiums when your income is above a set threshold.
How is MAGI calculated?
Modified Adjusted Gross Income is your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) plus any tax-exempt interest income. Social Security uses MAGI to decide whether IRMAA applies.
Why does Medicare use income from two years ago?
Because that's the most recent federal tax return the Social Security Administration has on file. For 2026 premiums, they generally use your 2024 tax return.
Can IRMAA be appealed?
Yes. If a life-changing event reduced your income, you can request a reconsideration by filing Form SSA-44 with Social Security.
What life events qualify for reconsideration?
Marriage, divorce, death of a spouse, work stoppage or reduction (such as retirement), loss of pension, and loss of income-producing property are common qualifying events.
Does everyone pay IRMAA?
No. Most people pay only the standard Part B premium. IRMAA applies only to higher-income beneficiaries above the annual income thresholds.

This calculator provides estimates only and is not an official Medicare determination. Premium amounts change annually. Consult Medicare.gov, Social Security, or a licensed Medicare agent for current information.
